ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific Burmese chicken salad (thoke) entry exists in FoodOn; closest_parent 'chicken salad food product' (FOODON_00002473) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Burmese-style chicken salad (thoke). A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) used as the closest defensible parent; the salad dressing and vegetable components are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese chicken salad or Myanmar thoke-style salad.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese chicken salad is approximately 250 g, typically presented as a plate of shredded poached chicken tossed with aromatics, lime dressing, and roasted chickpea powder.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken salad, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 01140 components) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table analogous estimation for Burmese-style chicken thoke
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese chicken thoke (kyat tha thoke) exists in any approved primary database. Nutrient values are estimated by component-level analysis: poached chicken breast (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 171477), onion, tomato, coriander, fish sauce, lime juice, roasted chickpea powder, and sesame oil, weighted to a representative recipe proportion. Values are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous mixed chicken salad entries. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed chicken salad with legume powder and vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese chicken thoke. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous mixed protein-vegetable salad with legume powder component. Chicken protein and fat suppress glycemic response; roasted chickpea powder (pe kyaw) has a GI of approximately 28–36 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for chickpea-based foods. Composite dish GI estimated conservatively at 35. GL per 250 g serving = (35 × 5.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 4.6.

hypertension.guidance: Each 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing about 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or substitute with low-sodium soy sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 35 and 13.5 g protein per 100 g, Burmese chicken salad produces a modest glycemic load of approximately 4.6 per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugary condiments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with moderate unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean poached chicken with a favourable unsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio (approximately 3:1) and beneficial micronutrients including potassium and vitamin B6; however, the sodium load from fish sauce warrants monitoring in patients with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burmese chicken salad (ကြက်သားသုပ်, romanized: Kyat Tha Thoke) is a traditional Myanmar cold salad dish made from poached or boiled chicken — typically breast or thigh — that is hand-shredded and tossed with thinly sliced onion, tomato, green chilli, fresh coriander, fried garlic, dried chilli flakes, fish sauce, lime juice, sesame oil, and roasted chickpea powder (pe kyaw). The dish is served at room temperature or slightly chilled and is a staple of Burmese cuisine both in Myanmar and in diaspora restaurants such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant in Singapore. The texture is tender and slightly chewy from the shredded chicken, with a bright, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile balanced by the nuttiness of sesame oil and chickpea powder. Nutritionally, the dish is high in lean protein (~13.5 g/100 g), moderate in fat (~4.8 g/100 g, predominantly unsaturated), and low in carbohydrates (~5.2 g/100 g), yielding approximately 118 kcal per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it appropriate for glycaemic management. The primary nutritional concern is sodium content (~420 mg/100 g) derived from fish sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal when prepared with certified fish sauce. It is commonly served as a starter or light main course and pairs well with steamed rice or Burmese mohinga soup.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese chicken thoke (kyat tha thoke) exists in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA). Nutrient values are estimated by component-level analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for individual ingredients weighted to a representative recipe. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for chickpea-based foods and mixed protein-vegetable salads. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central (poached chicken breast FDC ID 171477, fish sauce, sesame oil, onion, tomato, lime juice, roasted chickpea powder) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous entries. No direct database entry exists for this dish. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources for this dish type and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for the primary ingredients (poached chicken breast, plant-based oils, vegetables). 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Burmese chicken thoke was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 5. The input record listed food_group as 'Grains and staples' and cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which appear to be tagging errors from the field survey platform; this record has been reclassified to 'Meat and alternatives' based on the dish's primary ingredient (chicken) and preparation style. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and carry medium confidence. 7. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation; individual restaurant certification should be verified.
